one_line_takeaway: Echo Chamber is a black-box multi-turn jailbreak that plants benign-looking seeds and then coaxes the model to elaborate on its own prior output, achieving a 45.0% overall attack success rate across 7 models and 12 AdvBench tasks versus 28.6% for Crescendo and 9.5% for DAN.
key_finding: Across the 12 AdvBench tasks and 7 target models, Echo Chamber's overall attack success rate was 45.0%, versus 28.6% for Crescendo and 9.5% for DAN. Per-model highlights from Table 3: Gemini 2.5 Flash 72.7% (Echo Chamber) vs 33.3% (Crescendo) vs 25.0% (DAN); GPT-4.1 50.0% vs 16.7% vs 0.0%; Gemini 2.0 Flash 58.3% vs 50.0% vs 25.0%; GPT-4.1 mini 54.5% vs 25.0% vs 0.0%; Gemini 2.5 Pro 45.5% vs 33.3% vs 16.7%; GPT-5 mini 18.2% vs 16.7% vs 0.0%; GPT-5 nano 16.7% vs 25.0% vs 0.0%. Per-category (Table 4): Echo Chamber led on Violence (55.0% vs 19.0% Crescendo vs 0.0% DAN) and Hacking (50.0% vs 28.6% vs 9.5%), but Crescendo outperformed Echo Chamber on Fraud/Manipulation (Echo Chamber 50.0% vs Crescendo 66.7% vs DAN 14.3%); on Misinformation Echo Chamber scored 25.0% vs Crescendo's 0.0% vs DAN's 14.3%. At the individual-task level (Table 5), Echo Chamber hit 100% on the 'Weapon' (assassination-weapon design) task and 85.7% on 'CorpHack' (corporate network hacking), while Crescendo led on 'Manipulation' and 'IdTheft2' (both 85.7%); both attacks scored 0.0% on 'Virus'. In the smaller manual test (Table 2), Echo Chamber jailbroke all 5 models on the racist-manifesto and unsafe-vaccines tasks, and 4 of 5 (all except GPT-4.1) on the Molotov cocktail task.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Echo Chamber, a new black-box multi-turn LLM jailbreak that exploits a model's completion/consistency bias rather than explicit reframing or backtracking. The attack proceeds in five steps performed by the paper's own authors: (1) plant 'poisonous seeds' -- benign-looking keywords/context that implicitly carry the harmful concept without triggering safety filters; (2) plant 'steering seeds' that push the model toward a desired output format (e.g., a 'manual') independent of the harmful content itself; (3) 'invoke poisonous content' by requesting multiple candidate answers ('paths') from the model in one turn; (4) 'path selection', where the attacker manually or automatically picks the response fragment most aligned with the malicious objective; (5) a 'persuasion cycle' in which the attacker asks the model to elaborate on its own prior output (e.g., 'can you elaborate on the second paragraph'), leveraging the model's tendency toward self-consistency to amplify the toxic content turn over turn. The authors also built and released an automated version of the attack (one LLM generating attack turns, a second LLM acting as judge) and an open-source tool/dataset at github.com/NeuralTrust/echo-chamber.
threat_model: Fully black-box: the attacker has no access to model weights, architecture, or gradients, and interacts with the target purely through normal conversational turns (chat UI or API). The only requirement for the automated variant is programmatic (API) access to the target model. The attacker is assumed able to craft suggestive prompts and to identify which parts of the target's own responses can be reused to steer the conversation toward the malicious objective -- an assumption the paper explicitly attributes to prior work ([3] Chao et al. and [26] Chain of Attack).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the attack is inherently multi-turn, so systems without conversation history are more resistant, though the authors note most modern chat systems retain history; (2) the automated tool requires programmatic/API access to the target; (3) the automated pipeline itself depends on an LLM (Gemini 2.5 Pro) as both attacker and judge, inheriting that model's own alignment behavior -- the attacker LLM sometimes refuses or resists generating attack content or performing evaluation; (4) the authors explicitly flag that broader testing across a wider range of objectives and categories is needed to fully assess the attack's implications; (5) LLM-as-judge evaluation can produce false positives, which the authors partially mitigate with a two-stage (primary + secondary) judge design and manual spot-checking of top prompts, but do not report a quantified false-positive/agreement rate.

datasets_used: AdvBench (12 tasks selected via the Crescendo paper's protocol, grouped into Violence, Hacking, Fraud, and Misinformation categories, 3 tasks per category)
quantitative_results: Overall attack success rate across 12 tasks / 7 models: Echo Chamber 45.0%, Crescendo 28.6%, DAN 9.5%
Gemini 2.5 Flash: Echo Chamber 72.7%, Crescendo 33.3%, DAN 25.0%
Gemini 2.0 Flash: Echo Chamber 58.3%, Crescendo 50.0%, DAN 25.0%
Category Violence: Echo Chamber 55.0%, Crescendo 19.0%, DAN 0.0%
Category Hacking: Echo Chamber 50.0%, Crescendo 28.6%, DAN 9.5%
Category Fraud: Echo Chamber 50.0%, Crescendo 66.7%, DAN 14.3%
Category Misinformation: Echo Chamber 25.0%, Crescendo 0.0%, DAN 14.3%

Manual test (5 models x 3 tasks, max 4 attempts each): Echo Chamber succeeded on all 5 models for racist-manifesto and unsafe-vaccines tasks; succeeded on 4/5 models (all but GPT-4.1) for the Molotov-cocktail task
baselines_compared: Crescendo (multi-turn jailbreak, Russinovich et al., USENIX Security 2025)
DAN / Do Anything Now (single-turn, static, in-the-wild jailbreak, Shen et al., USENIX Security 2024)
